examples.title: Algorithm: Token Recognition Using Regular Expressions
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how a lexical analyzer recognizes tokens by matching input against regular expression patterns
examples.code:
ALGORITHM TokenRecognizer
INPUT: source_code (character stream)
OUTPUT: list of (token_type, lexeme) pairs

BEGIN
 SET position = 0
 SET tokens = empty list
 SET source = remove_whitespace(source_code)
 
 WHILE position < length(source) DO
 SET matched = FALSE
 
 // Try each token pattern in order of priority
 FOR EACH pattern IN token_patterns DO
 SET match_result = match_pattern(source, position, pattern)
 
 IF match_result.success THEN
 IF match_result.length > 0 THEN
 tokens.append((pattern.type, match_result.lexeme))
 position = match_result.new_position
 matched = TRUE
 BREAK
 END IF
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 IF NOT matched THEN
 ERROR "Illegal character at position " + position
 position = position + 1 // Skip illegal character
 END IF
 END WHILE
 
 RETURN tokens
END

FUNCTION match_pattern(source, pos, pattern)
INPUT: source string, current position, pattern definition
OUTPUT: match result with lexeme and new position
BEGIN
 SET regex = compile_regex(pattern.regex)
 SET match = regex.match(source, pos)
 
 IF match AND match.start() == pos THEN
 RETURN (success=TRUE, lexeme=match.group(), 
 length=match.end()-match.start(), 
 new_position=match.end())
 ELSE
 RETURN (success=FALSE)
 END IF
END

// Helper function to define token patterns
FUNCTION define_tokens()
BEGIN
 RETURN [
 (type="KEYWORD", regex="if|else|while|return|int|float"),
 (type="IDENTIFIER", regex="[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9]*"),
 (type="INTEGER", regex="[0-9]+"),
 (type="REAL", regex="[0-9]+\\.[0-9]+"),
 (type="OPERATOR", regex="<=|>=|==|!=|\\+|-|\\*|/"),
 (type="PUNCTUATION", regex=";|,|\\(|\\)|{|}")
 ]
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the token recognition process in a lexical analyzer. It iterates through the source code, attempting to match each position against predefined token patterns defined by regular expressions. The algorithm follows the longest match principle - it finds the longest matching pattern at each position. If no pattern matches, it reports an error and skips the illegal character. The token_patterns list is ordered by priority, with earlier rules having higher precedence.

examples.title: Python: Token Specification Using Regular Expressions
examples.description: Implementation showing how to define and use regular expressions for specifying tokens in a programming language
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates token specification using regular expressions. The TOKEN_SPECIFICATIONS list defines all token types with their corresponding regex patterns. The LexicalAnalyzer class iterates through the source code, attempting to match each position against the token patterns in order of priority. It implements the longest match principle by breaking after the first match (the patterns are ordered so that more specific patterns come first). Whitespace and comments are filtered out and not included in the token stream. The implementation handles keywords, identifiers, numbers, strings, operators, and punctuation.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n * m) where n is source length and m is number of token patterns
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing tokens and source code

examples.title: C: Simple Lexical Analyzer with Token Specifications
examples.description: Implementation of a basic lexical analyzer in C that recognizes tokens based on regular expression patterns
examples.explanation: This C implementation provides a complete lexical analyzer demonstrating token specification concepts. It shows how regular expression patterns are implemented in practice for recognizing different token types. The analyzer handles: identifiers and keywords (letter followed by letters/digits), integers and real numbers (digit sequences with optional decimal point), string literals (characters between quotes), operators (single and two-character), and punctuation. It implements the longest match principle by checking for two-character operators before single-character ones. The code also demonstrates proper handling of whitespace, single-line and multi-line comments, and error reporting with line numbers.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the length of input source code
examples.spaceComplexity: O(m) where m is maximum token length plus input buffer size
